figures.caption: Table 1. Comparison of object-level AUC-ROC results (%) of various methods on the Anomaly-ShapeNet dataset.

Figure 3. Qualitative comparison on three datasets (green letters): our crafted industrial components (1st–2nd rows), Real3D-AD (3rd), and Anomaly-ShapeNet (4th). The dashed line indicates different object classes. Representative anomaly types (in blue) include displacement, bending, concavity, sinks, and bulges. Anomaly points are highlighted in red. Ground truth uses red masks (first two rows) or red contours (last two rows) to indicate anomaly regions overlaid with an anomalous mesh. Each column shows results from a specific method.

Figure 2. Overview of the proposed shape anomaly detection framework. The pipeline processes a normal shape S through several modules: Patchification segments the input into patches {Sj}, while Negative Augmentation generates pseudo anomalous shapes S̃ that are also patchified during training (red regions indicate anomalies); (a) Adaptive Patch Feature Extraction computes features for each patch by averaging its points and querying a pre-trained UNet to obtain patch features {pj}; (b) Template Codebook stores a dictionary of normal template patch features {tk}, from which each patch retrieves its closest matching patch; (c) Point to Patch Cross-attention fuses the template features {tk} with point-level features extracted from the pre-trained UNet encoder through multi-head attention mechanism; (d) Patch Score Modulation modulates the cross-attention outputs using similarity scores between normal and abnormal patch features {tk}, {pj}, with the modulated features fed into MLP regression layers to predict the anomaly score δpred. During training, (e) the loss is computed as ô−ogt, where ogt represents the ground-truth offset direction derived from the difference S− S̃. During inference (indicated by green arrows), only the test shape is input for anomaly detection.
